episode_meta.title: Rune Ytreberg & Lars Adrian Giske: Building AI Tools for Investigative Journalism in Local News

ai_tools: Property Bot (alert system for property transactions)
LAILA (AI research assistant / cobot for data-driven journalism)
Fisheries Database (AI anomaly detection on 5 million fish transactions)
GIN (AI-powered system ranking municipal documents by newsworthiness)
Domain-specific AI research assistants (RAG-based chat with FOIA documents)
OCR assistant (converting PDF images to text for ingestion)
Python script for PDF-to-JSON conversion and forced prompting for timelines
Slack notification/alert system (built on top of GIN)

use_cases.description: Automated ingestion of nightly property transactions with alert system notifying journalists of newsworthy transactions across 70 local newspapers
use_cases.outcome: Produces several news stories per week; convinced Polaris Media leadership to fund further AI development

use_cases.description: Anomaly and pattern detection across 5 million fish transactions over 20 years to flag non-sustainable fishing activities
use_cases.outcome: Detected a company engaged in non-sustainable practices; iTromso was the only newspaper able to film the police and Coast Guard raid because they already knew the target from their AI system

use_cases.description: AI-powered ranking and summarization of municipal zoning/planning documents to surface newsworthy items from hundreds of daily PDFs
use_cases.outcome: Reduced document review time from 60-90 minutes to 5 minutes per day; two summer interns delivered five cover stories in their first week; lowered threshold for junior journalists to cover complex beats

use_cases.description: Investigation of systemic understaffing at the University Hospital of Northern Norway using FOIA documents analyzed via a RAG system with health investigation system prompts
use_cases.tool_used: Domain-specific AI research assistant (vector database + RAG)
use_cases.outcome: Discovered an X-ray crisis where at least five people died due to a freelance doctor who spent insufficient time reviewing X-ray images over many years; work that would have taken two to three journalists many weeks was done in seconds

use_cases.description: Collaborative cross-border investigation with Swedish newspaper Goteborgs-Posten requiring timeline generation from large document corpus
use_cases.tool_used: Python script for PDF-to-JSON conversion combined with batch prompting to OpenAI for timeline extraction
use_cases.outcome: Successfully generated timeline of events from hundreds of documents after overcoming RAG limitations on aggregation tasks

use_cases.description: Analysis of annual financial reports to detect significant changes and alert journalists
use_cases.outcome: Journalists receive alerts when significant changes appear in new annual reports compared to historical data

key_claims.claim: 80% of the time a journalist spends on a story is spent on initial research and finding the story
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

key_claims.claim: 50% of all correspondence between municipalities and inhabitants of Norway is about urban development or planning and zoning
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

key_claims.claim: 60% of municipalities in Norway publish all needed zoning/planning data openly, leaving 40% that do not
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

key_claims.claim: 80% of Norwegians own their own house, making urban development and zoning a critical beat for local newspapers
key_claims.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

key_claims.claim: GIN reduced daily document review from 60-90 minutes to 5 minutes for journalists
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

key_claims.claim: Two summer interns fresh out of journalism school delivered five cover stories in their first week using GIN
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

key_claims.claim: The AI newsworthiness model trained on journalist gut-feeling (thumbs up/down) was better at capturing relevant entities than manually curated entity lists
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske / Rune Ytreberg

key_claims.claim: Fish is the second largest industry after oil in northern Norway, with significant non-sustainable activity
key_claims.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

key_claims.claim: Four municipalities decided to start publishing data openly after seeing the value of the GIN tool, increasing government transparency
key_claims.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

key_claims.claim: At least five people died due to an X-ray doctor who did not spend enough time reviewing images, discovered through their AI-assisted FOIA document analysis
key_claims.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

challenges.description: 40% of Norwegian municipalities do not publish zoning and planning data openly, limiting GIN's coverage to only 130 of Norway's municipalities and roughly half of Polaris newsrooms
Plethora of different municipal systems, web portals, and data providers with no standardized format across Norway

Municipal document portals have poor UI with minimal metadata -- titles are often uninformative (e.g., just 'referencing project'), forcing journalists to manually sift through 400-500 documents daily
RAG systems cannot reliably perform aggregation tasks such as generating timelines across an entire document corpus due to context window limits and how vector database querying works
Risk of hallucination in LLM outputs, including hallucinated source citations, requiring careful fact-checking and journalist-in-the-loop verification
Many FOIA documents were PDF images rather than searchable text, requiring an OCR preprocessing step before they could be ingested into the AI system

Prompting quality varied significantly between a 21-year-old developer and a 30-year veteran investigative journalist, producing very different outputs -- highlighting the need for editorial-developer collaboration
When expert journalists are sick or on vacation, local newsrooms lose critical beat coverage because institutional knowledge resides in individuals rather than systems
Small local newsroom (25-person team) competing against a competitor twice their size, requiring extreme efficiency

quotes.text: We started with low hanging fruits. That was very easy to get our hands on and that we could very efficiently get the product out and show our editor that this is actually working and it's going to have an impact. It's going to produce better journalism.
quotes.speaker: Rune Ytreberg
quotes.topic: strategy for AI adoption in newsrooms
quotes.pedagogical_value: Demonstrates a pragmatic, iterative approach to introducing AI in newsrooms -- start with simple, high-impact tools to build editorial buy-in

quotes.text: All the police published was a short little notification saying there is an ongoing raid at a fisheries plant in northern Norway. That was all we had. But we knew exactly where to go. So we were there ten minutes after the police and were able to film it, do interviews. Nobody had access like we did.
quotes.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
quotes.pedagogical_value: Vivid example of how AI anomaly detection gave a small local newsroom an exclusive investigative edge that no competitor could match

quotes.text: It's gone from spending an hour to an hour and a half sitting and manually doing this work to spending five minutes doing it. That's a huge timesaver... every day when you come to work, you have the day's stories ready for you. You just have to start calling sources.
quotes.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
quotes.pedagogical_value: Quantifies the concrete efficiency gains of AI tools in daily journalism workflow, showing how AI shifts journalist time from research to reporting

quotes.text: Nobody else is as capable of asking the right questions in a methodical way... And verify the answers. And be able to very easily detect okay this is a hallucination because this information here is not part of the document because we are very good at verifying outputs.
quotes.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
quotes.topic: journalists as ideal users of generative AI
quotes.pedagogical_value: Argues that journalistic skills -- questioning, verification, source-checking -- make journalists uniquely well-suited to work with and audit AI systems

quotes.text: The knowledge that the senior journalists have on their beat is somehow transferred into the system. So now fresh journalists will learn from the ranking of the documents. The knowledge from the senior journalist is put inside the system so new journalists can learn from the old journalists, even if they have never met each other before.
quotes.speaker: Rune Ytreberg
quotes.topic: codifying editorial expertise into AI systems
quotes.pedagogical_value: Illustrates how AI can serve as a vehicle for institutional knowledge transfer, addressing the critical problem of expertise loss in small newsrooms

quotes.text: I think it's all about combining complementary methods. Be they gen AI or machine learning or traditional just if-and-then coding. Once you start seeing how they can complement each other and building systems that integrate all of these approaches, that's when you get something really useful.
quotes.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
quotes.pedagogical_value: Advocates against treating LLMs as a silver bullet; emphasizes that the most effective journalism AI systems combine multiple techniques including traditional programming, ML, and generative AI

recommendations.advice: Start with low-hanging fruits that demonstrate clear value to editors, then scale from there once you have buy-in and funding
recommendations.speaker: Rune Ytreberg
recommendations.target_audience: newsroom leaders and data journalists starting AI adoption

recommendations.advice: Build domain-specific AI tools rather than general-purpose ones to reduce hallucination risk and increase accuracy of alerts and analysis
recommendations.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

recommendations.advice: Always keep a journalist in the loop -- AI systems should assist, not replace, editorial judgment and fact-checking
recommendations.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

recommendations.advice: Combine generative AI with traditional machine learning, vector databases, and conventional programming rather than relying on LLMs alone
recommendations.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
recommendations.target_audience: newsroom developers and AI product teams

recommendations.advice: Pair developers and journalists closely when building AI tools -- developers bring prompting technique expertise while journalists bring editorial domain knowledge; both are essential
recommendations.speaker: Rune Ytreberg / Lars Adrian Giske

recommendations.advice: Model journalist expertise (gut feeling) through training data rather than hardcoding rules or entity lists -- let the AI learn patterns from expert editorial judgment
recommendations.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
recommendations.target_audience: newsroom AI developers building newsworthiness models

recommendations.advice: Seek out strategic partners and collaborators (like IBM) through media hubs and networks -- small teams cannot build everything alone
recommendations.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske
recommendations.target_audience: small local newsrooms with limited technical resources

recommendations.advice: Be aware of RAG system limitations, especially for aggregation tasks like timeline generation; supplement with batch processing and traditional database approaches
recommendations.speaker: Rune Ytreberg / Lars Adrian Giske
recommendations.target_audience: journalists and developers using RAG for document analysis

external_references: Polaris Media (Scandinavian media house owning 70+ local newspapers)
IBM (co-creation workshop partner for GIN development)
OpenAI platform (used for building initial AI assistants)
Anthropic LLM (used as alternative to OpenAI for document analysis)
Goteborgs-Posten (Swedish investigative newspaper, cross-border collaboration partner)

Harvard Innovation Labs (supporting Newsroom Robots podcast via Spark grant)
Norwegian Innovation Research Council (provided funding for LAILA project in 2022)
University Hospital of Northern Norway (subject of understaffing investigation)
Norwegian FOIA web portal system (public records request system)
